title: Patience
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Context: The Fruit of the Spirit in Community
subpoints: The Galatians were being divided by a false teacher requiring law-keeping alongside faith
Fruit of the Spirit are virtues manifested in relationships within the body of Christ
Spirituality in Galatians 6:1 is defined by love, joy, peace, and patience — not Bible reading or prayer length alone

outline.title: Patience as a Communicable Attribute of God
subpoints: Greek makrothumia: forbearance toward people; literally 'be quick to be slow'
God revealed Himself to Moses as long-suffering (Exodus 34:6)
Two sides of God's patience: leading to repentance (Romans 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9) and restraining wrath (Romans 9:22; Nahum 1:2-3)
Parable of the unforgiving servant: patience is a test of genuine faith (Matthew 18:23-35)

outline.title: What Patience Is
subpoints: Being slow to anger; forbearance and putting up with others
Opposite of irritability; companion of humility, love, and peace (Ephesians 4:1-2)
The ruling principle for relating to everyone in the church (1 Thessalonians 5:14)
Patience is cultivated by practicing knowledge of God's Word (Colossians 1:9-12)

outline.title: Patience as a Mark of Maturity and Mission
subpoints: Patience is a characteristic of growth in faith (Hebrews 5)
Patience qualifies us as evangelists at home and abroad (2 Corinthians 6:4)
Patience is a mark of godly preaching and godly parenting (Colossians 3:12)

outline.title: Why We Should Be Patient
subpoints: Because God is patient with us (2 Peter 3:9)
Because love is patient — it is the first characteristic of love (1 Corinthians 13)
Because Jesus is coming back and will judge all things (James 5:8-9)

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the series on the fruit of the Spirit by examining patience (makrothumia), set in the context of the Galatian church's internal conflict over false teaching. He argues that the fruit of the Spirit are not private virtues but relational graces designed to be expressed in the community of believers. Patience is first identified as a communicable attribute of God: He revealed Himself to Moses as long-suffering (Exodus 34:6), and His patience operates in two modes — graciously leading sinners to repentance (Romans 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9) and restraining His wrath against the unrepentant (Romans 9:22; Nahum 1:2-3). Through the parable of the unforgiving servant, Pastor Lyro shows that patience is a test of genuine faith: only those who have truly experienced forgiveness can extend it. Biblically, patience means being slow to anger, bearing with others even when wronged, and is the opposite of irritability. It grows through a practiced knowledge of God and is a hallmark of genuine spiritual maturity. The sermon closes with three reasons to be patient: God's own patience with us, love's patient nature, and the imminent return of Christ who stands at the door as judge.
transcript:
## Introduction: Fruit of the Spirit in Community

We continue our series on the fruit of the Spirit. The fruit of the Spirit are virtues that manifest themselves in relationship — as the people of God living in community.

The Galation Christians were experiencing some trouble. A false teacher had come in and was dividing them. The teaching was that in order to be a true follower of Jesus Christ, faith in Christ was not enough — you had to also follow the law of God. And some people had bought into this idea. And now they are fighting among each other.

We see in verse 15 that they are biting one another, and in verse 26, as they are going to devour one another, if they are not controlled by the Spirit.

The fruit of the Spirit are given to us — cultivated in our hearts so that we can manifest them in the community of Christ. The one who walks in the Spirit loves his brothers and sisters in Christ. The one who walks in the Spirit is also patient with them. If you look at Galatians 6 verse 1, Paul says, "Brethren, if a man is overtaken in any trespass, you who are spiritual" — who are the spiritual ones? The ones who are loving, who have joy, who are at peace with the brethren, who are patient with one another. That is the sign of spirituality.

So we considered love, joy, and peace. Now let us consider patience.

## Patience as a Communicable Attribute of God

The first thing I want us to see is that patience — in our list, the word is long-suffering — is a communicable attribute of God that God shares with us.

Now there are two words in the New Testament for patience. One is hupomone, which means to bear under difficult circumstances — that's patience with circumstances. The second one is makrothumia, which relates to forbearing towards people. And that's the one that's in our passage today. It literally means "be quick to be slow" — be quick to be slow in the way that you relate to people.

This is the one that's an attribute of God, because God relates to His covenant people with patience. In Exodus 34:6, the Lord passed before Moses and proclaimed: "The Lord, the Lord God, merciful and gracious, long-suffering..." One of the ways that God wants us to know Him is as the God of patience.

There are two sides to God's patience. The first is that it shows itself in repentance on the part of the sinner and in forgiveness on the part of God. Romans 2:4 says: "Or do you despise the riches of His goodness, forbearance, and long-suffering, not knowing that the goodness of God leads you to repentance?" And we all know 2 Peter 3:9: "The Lord is not slack concerning His promise, as some count slackness, but is long-suffering toward us, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance."

The other side of patience is restraint. When God exercises patience, His wrath is not eliminated but postponed. In Romans 9:22, Paul says: "What if God, wanting to show His wrath and to make His power known, endured with much long-suffering the vessels of wrath prepared for destruction?" And in Nahum 1:2-3, these two ideas of patience are brought together: "God is jealous and the Lord avenges... the Lord is slow to anger and great in power."

In the parable of the unforgiving servant (Matthew 18:23-35), the king forgives an enormous debt, but the forgiven servant refuses to forgive a much smaller debt. The king's patience equaled forgiveness. The expectation is that those who have experienced God's patience in the form of forgiveness will also be patient with others. Jesus makes patience a test of genuine faith.

## What Is Patience?

Pre-Christian, non-biblical writings treated patience as a negative word — something to be done when nothing else could be done. But biblical literature has always seen patience as a virtue. A good definition of patience is being slow to anger.

Patience is forbearance, long-suffering, putting up with others. It is the exact opposite of irritability. Patience is a companion of humility, of love, and of peace — they all go together. In Ephesians 4:1-2, Paul says: "Walk worthy of the calling with which you were called, with all lowliness and gentleness, with long-suffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace."

Patience is a ruling principle in the way that we relate to everyone in the church. In 1 Thessalonians 5:14, Paul says: "We exhort you, brethren, warn those who are unruly, comfort the fainthearted, uphold the weak, be patient with all."

Brothers and sisters, patience is cultivated by growing in our knowledge of God — a particular kind of knowledge that involves practicing what you know. In Colossians 1:9-12, the apostle prays for the Colossian church: "We do not cease to pray for you... that you may walk worthy of the Lord, fully pleasing Him, being fruitful in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God, strengthened with all might according to His glorious power, for all patience and long-suffering with joy."

## Patience as a Mark of Maturity and Mission

Patience is a characteristic of growth in faith. The author of Hebrews says: "Be those who through faith and patience inherit the promise." If we are growing in Christ, we also are growing in patience.

Patience is also an evangelistic qualification, both for home and abroad. In 2 Corinthians 6:4, Paul says: "In all things we commend ourselves as ministers of God... by long-suffering." And patience must be a characteristic of those who preach the gospel even amid opposition (2 Timothy 4:2).

Lastly, patience is a mark of godly parenting. In Colossians 3:12, put on patience. Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged — that is a demonstration of patience.

## Why Should We Be Patient?

The biggest reason to be patient is because God is patient with us. "The Lord is not slack concerning His promise... but is long-suffering toward us" (2 Peter 3:9).

Also because we love one another. Patience is the first characteristic of love. In 1 Corinthians 13, the very first thing Paul lists as a characteristic of love is that love is patient.

And because Jesus is coming back. In James 5:8, James says: "You also be patient. Establish your hearts, for the coming of the Lord is at hand." Every day He does not return is a day in which souls are being added to the kingdom of God. And in James 5:9: "Do not grumble against one another, brethren, lest you be condemned. Behold, the judge is standing at the door."

## Conclusion

God calls us to be patient because that's a fruit of the Spirit. We need this fruit so that we can live in harmony in the body of Christ. We need this fruit so that we can live in harmony in our own families. Our God graciously grows patience in us as we live in obedience to what His Spirit says to us in the Bible.

Father, thank you that you are a God who is patient with us. We thank you that in your patience you lead us to repent, and we pray that those who don't know you today would come to know Jesus Christ as the one who suffered for them. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
